message.content:
Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Checkpointer bootstrap holds the process-wide _init_lock across a blocking, unbounded cross-process pg_advisory_lock + setup() — head-of-line blocking that can stall ALL checkpointer initialization (even for unrelated databases) indefinitely
DIMENSION: concurrency & load
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py 195-211
DESCRIPTION: get_checkpointer acquires the single process-global _init_lock (line 195) and, while holding it, performs blocking I/O: pool.connection() (line 197), SELECT pg_advisory_lock(...) (line 198), and PostgresSaver(...).setup() DDL (line 203). pg_advisory_lock is a SESSION-level lock that blocks INDEFINITELY if another process already holds the same key (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY is a single fixed constant for all schemas). Because _init_lock is a single process-wide mutex (not keyed by conn_str), one thread blocked inside this section — e.g. waiting on the advisory lock held by a stuck or crashed peer process — blocks EVERY other thread's get_checkpointer call, including initialization for entirely UNRELATED databases / conn_strs that have nothing to do with the contended lock. Under concurrent run_study invocations that each build a checkpointer, this serializes all bootstrap and can wedge the whole process on a single peer's stuck advisory lock. PoC confirmed: a thread mirroring the exact (with _init_lock: ... advisory-lock-wait) structure for db_A blocks a second thread initializing an unrelated db_B for the full duration of db_A's advisory-lock wait (db_B blocked ~0.62s on a wait that, in production, has no upper bound).
SUGGESTED FIX: Do not hold a single global mutex across blocking cross-process I/O. Either (a) replace _init_lock with a per-conn_str in-process lock (dict of locks keyed by conn_str, guarded by a tiny meta-lock) so different databases bootstrap concurrently and only same-conn_str threads serialize; and/or (b) acquire pg_advisory_lock with a bounded timeout (pg_advisory_lock with a statement_timeout, or use pg_try_advisory_lock with retry/backoff) so a stuck peer cannot hang init forever; and only hold the in-process lock around the in-process check-and-set of _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS, not around the DDL/advisory-lock wait.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze this checkpointer bootstrap concurrency claim. Let me read the actual code.

content.content:
1	"""Durable run-state checkpointer — local, data-sovereign (ADR-0003).
2	
3	The orchestration graph persists its full state (current phase, gate decisions, locked
4	hypotheses, verdicts) through a checkpointer so a run can interrupt at a human-approval gate
5	and resume later. That state holds the entire analysis, so the store is **on-box only**
6	(ADR-0003); a SaaS checkpointer is disqualified.
7	
8	This module is the LangGraph checkpointer factory, ported faithfully from the prior
9	platform's hardened implementation and generalized (dataset-agnostic ``ASSAY_*`` env, no
10	config-file coupling — see ADR-0006). The hardened behaviors are deliberately preserved
11	because each prevents a real production failure:
12	
13	- a **self-healing ConnectionPool** (not a raw connection): when Postgres goes away — node
14	  sleep, restart, idle timeout, a network blip — a cached raw connection is permanently
15	  broken and every later checkpoint op raises, i.e. durability is lost exactly during the
16	  interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to support. The pool runs a liveness check on
17	  checkout and replaces dead connections;
18	- a **session advisory lock** serializing first-time schema setup across *processes*, run on
19	  the *same* connection as the DDL (a pool would otherwise check out a different connection,
20	  leaving the lock ineffective);
21	- a **once-per-process-per-conn guard** (``setup()`` is idempotent but re-locking + replaying
22	  the migration query on every call is wasteful), with an in-process lock making the
23	  check-and-set atomic so concurrent threads don't both run setup;
24	- **credential redaction**: a backend exception (psycopg/psycopg_pool) can embed the full
25	  ``user:password`` URI/DSN in its own message, so the conn string is scrubbed precisely and
26	  the exception text via URI-userinfo + DSN-password backstops; the redacted message is then
27	  raised *outside* the exception handler, so the credential-bearing original is retained as
28	  neither ``__cause__`` nor ``__context__`` (a logger walking ``__context__`` cannot
29	  re-expose it);
30	- a single shared **atexit** pool-cleanup handler (one per process, not one per call) so the
31	  pool's worker thread is joined before interpreter finalization.
32	
33	LangGraph/psycopg are optional (the ``persistence`` extra) and imported lazily; the
34	credential-redaction and connection-resolution logic is pure and unit-tested offline.
35	"""
36	
37	from __future__ import annotations
38	
39	import atexit
40	import os
41	import re
42	import threading
43	from typing import Any, cast
44	from urllib.parse import urlsplit
45	
46	from assay_engine._local import require_local_uri
47	
48	_DEFAULT_PG = "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay"
49	
50	# Arbitrary fixed key for the Postgres session advisory lock that serializes checkpoint
51	# schema migrations across concurrent initializers. The value is irrelevant beyond being a
52	# stable per-logical-schema constant; "ASSY" as an int.
53	_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY = 0x4153_5359
54	
55	# Guards the process-local one-time-init bookkeeping. The PG advisory lock serializes DDL
56	# across PROCESSES; this lock makes the in-process check-and-set sequences (atexit
57	# registration, the initialized-conn set) atomic so two threads can't double-register or
58	# double-setup.
59	_init_lock = threading.Lock()
60	
61	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
62	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
63	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
64	_atexit_registered = False
65	
66	# conn_strs whose schema bootstrap (advisory lock + setup() DDL) has already run in this
67	# process — bootstrap once per process per schema; the advisory lock still guards the
68	# first-time cross-process race.
69	_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS: set[str] = set()
70	
71	# Strips ``://user:password@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
72	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
73	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
74	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[^@\s]+@")
75	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=...`` / ``pgpassword=...``),
76	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
77	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
78	
79	
80	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
81	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
82	
83	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
84	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
85	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
86	    """
87	    try:
88	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
89	        host = u.hostname or ""
90	        port = u.port
91	        scheme = u.scheme
92	        path = u.path
93	    except ValueError:
94	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
95	    if not host:
96	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
97	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
98	        host = f"[{host}]"
99	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
100	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
101	
102	
103	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
104	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
105	
106	    Layers, in order of precision:
107	
108	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
109	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
110	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://user:pass@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
111	       *other* URI in the text;
112	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=``/``pgpassword=`` strip for the keyword/value form.
113	
114	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
115	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
116	    """
117	    if conn_str:
118	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
119	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
120	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
121	
122	
123	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
124	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
125	
126	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
127	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
128	    """
129	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
130	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
131	
132	
133	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
134	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
135	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
136	    that can raise)."""
137	    try:
138	        pool.close()
139	    except Exception:
140	        pass
141	
142	
143	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
144	    for p in _OPEN_POOLS:
145	        _safe_close(p)
146	
147	
148	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
149	    global _atexit_registered
150	    with _init_lock:
151	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
152	        if not _atexit_registered:
153	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
154	            _atexit_registered = True
155	
156	
157	def get_checkpointer(use_memory: bool = False) -> Any:
158	    """Return a configured LangGraph checkpointer.
159	
160	    ``use_memory=True`` returns an in-memory saver (tests / no-Postgres). Otherwise a
161	    Postgres-backed saver over a self-healing pool, with advisory-locked one-time schema
162	    setup. Raises ``RuntimeError`` (with credentials redacted) if the backend is unreachable
163	    or the extra is not installed.
164	    """
165	    try:
166	        if use_memory:
167	            from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
168	
169	            return MemorySaver()
170	        from langgraph.checkpoint.postgres import PostgresSaver
171	        from psycopg.rows import dict_row
172	        from psycopg_pool import ConnectionPool
173	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
174	        raise RuntimeError(
175	            "Postgres checkpointer requires the 'persistence' extra "
176	            "(langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg[binary,pool]) — not installed"
177	        ) from exc
178	
179	    conn_str = get_postgres_connection_string()
180	    failure: str | None = None
181	    pool: Any = None
182	    try:
183	        pool = ConnectionPool(
184	            conn_str,
185	            min_size=1,
186	            max_size=8,
187	            kwargs={"autocommit": True, "prepare_threshold": 0, "row_factory": dict_row},
188	            check=ConnectionPool.check_connection,  # liveness check on checkout (self-healing)
189	            open=True,
190	        )
191	        _register_pool_cleanup(pool)
192	        # Bootstrap the schema once per process per conn_str. The advisory lock and the DDL
193	        # must run on the SAME connection (pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped); a pool-backed
194	        # setup() would lock one connection and DDL on another, leaving the lock ineffective.
195	        with _init_lock:
196	            if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS:
197	                with pool.connection() as conn:
198	                    conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
199	                    try:
200	                        # row_factory=dict_row makes this connection dict-rowed at runtime
201	                        # (what PostgresSaver requires); the cast asserts that runtime truth
202	                        # the type system can't infer from the pool kwargs.
203	                        PostgresSaver(cast(Any, conn)).setup()  # DDL on the locked connection
204	                    finally:
205	                        # Best-effort unlock; the lock auto-releases when the connection
206	                        # returns to the pool, and raising here would mask a setup() error.
207	                        try:
208	                            conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(%s)", (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY,))
209	                        except Exception:
210	                            pass
211	                _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS.add(conn_str)
212	        return PostgresSaver(cast(Any, pool))  # dict-rowed pool (row_factory=dict_row)
213	    except Exception as exc:
214	        # Build the redacted message here, but raise it OUTSIDE the handler (below) so the
215	        # original — credential-bearing — exception is retained as neither __cause__ NOR
216	        # __context__ (a logger walking __context__ would otherwise re-expose the password).
217	        failure = (
218	            f"Postgres checkpointer connection failed (is Postgres running on "
219	            f"{_sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)}?): {redact_creds(str(exc), conn_str)}. "
220	            "For tests without Postgres, use get_checkpointer(use_memory=True)."
221	        )
222	        # The pool may have opened before bootstrap failed — close it and drop it from the
223	        # cleanup registry so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread (#105).
224	        if pool is not None:
225	            _safe_close(pool)
226	            with _init_lock:
227	                if pool in _OPEN_POOLS:
228	                    _OPEN_POOLS.remove(pool)
229	    raise RuntimeError(failure)
230	
231	
232	def configured_checkpointer(use_memory: bool | None = None) -> Any:
233	    """Return the checkpointer the environment selects.
234	
235	    ``use_memory`` (or ``ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND=memory``) chooses the in-memory saver;
236	    otherwise Postgres.
237	    """
238	    if use_memory is None:
239	        use_memory = os.environ.get("ASSAY_CHECKPOINT_BACKEND", "postgres").lower() == "memory"
240	    return get_checkpointer(use_memory=use_memory)
241

content.text:
The code matches the described claim exactly. Let me analyze the structure carefully.

The claim has two parts:
1. `_init_lock` is a single process-wide mutex, held across blocking cross-process I/O (`pool.connection()`, `pg_advisory_lock`, `setup()`).
2. `pg_advisory_lock` is session-level, blocks indefinitely, and `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY` is a single fixed constant for all schemas/conn_strs.

Let me verify the key behavioral facts. First, is `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY` really a single constant shared across all conn_strs?

That's confirmed at line 53 — it's a module-level constant, same for every conn_str. So even a different database's bootstrap takes the same advisory lock key... but wait, advisory locks are per-database in Postgres. Let me think about whether the cross-database blocking claim holds.

Let me check how `get_checkpointer` is actually called concurrently in practice.

content.text:
Now let me reason about the technical correctness of the cross-database blocking claim, which is the core severity driver.

Key facts:
1. `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY` is a single constant (line 53).
2. But `pg_advisory_lock` keys are scoped **per database** in Postgres. Two different databases (different conn_strs pointing to different DBs) would NOT contend on the same advisory lock — they're separate lock namespaces. So the "stuck peer on db_A blocks db_B's advisory lock" is wrong at the Postgres level. However, the verifier's claim is more subtle: the `_init_lock` (in-process mutex) is what serializes db_A and db_B in the SAME process, regardless of whether their advisory locks contend.

The real claim: thread bootstrapping db_A holds `_init_lock` while blocked on db_A's advisory lock; thread bootstrapping db_B (totally unrelated) can't even acquire `_init_lock`, so it's blocked behind db_A even though their Postgres advisory locks would never contend.

This is the head-of-line blocking claim, and it's structurally accurate. Let me verify the realism: does the engine ever use multiple distinct conn_strs in one process? The conn_str comes only from `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` env, resolved fresh each call. Let me check the concurrent test.

content.content:
149	def test_postgres_concurrent_get_checkpointer(monkeypatch):
150	    # concurrent initializers must not race the advisory-locked one-time schema setup: the
151	    # _init_lock serializes the in-process check, the PG advisory lock the cross-process DDL.
152	    import threading
153	
154	    from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp
155	
156	    monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL", _pg_url())
157	    monkeypatch.setattr(cp, "_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS", set())  # force setup contention this run
158

160	    errors: list = []
161	
162	    def worker():
163	        try:
164	            results.append(cp.get_checkpointer(use_memory=False))
165	        except RuntimeError as exc:
166	            errors.append(exc)
167	
168	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker) for _ in range(8)]
169	    for t in threads:
170	        t.start()
171	    for t in threads:
172	        t.join(timeout=30)
173	
174	    if errors and not results:
175	        pytest.skip(f"postgres present but db not usable: {errors[0]}")
176	    assert not errors, f"concurrent get_checkpointer raised: {errors}"
177	    assert len(results) == 8  # every concurrent caller got a usable saver, no race failure
178

content.text: I have enough understanding of the fake harness. Let me write a PoC that drives the real `get_checkpointer` code with a `pool.connection()` whose `execute("pg_advisory_lock")` blocks (simulating a stuck peer), and a second thread initializing an unrelated conn_str.

input.content:
"""PoC: does holding _init_lock across the advisory-lock wait block an UNRELATED conn_str?

Drives the REAL get_checkpointer code with fake langgraph/psycopg modules, where the
advisory-lock execute() for db_A blocks. A second thread bootstraps an unrelated db_B.
If db_B is blocked for ~the duration of db_A's wait, head-of-line blocking is confirmed.
"""
import sys, types, threading, time

import os
os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = "postgresql://localhost:5432/dbA"

from assay_engine.persistence import checkpoint as cp

A_HOLD = 1.0  # seconds db_A's advisory lock "waits" (unbounded in prod)
block_release = threading.Event()


class FakeConn:
    def __init__(self, conn_str):
        self.conn_str = conn_str
    def execute(self, sql, params=None):
        if "pg_advisory_lock" in sql and "dbA" in self.conn_str:
            # simulate a stuck peer holding the cross-process advisory lock
            block_release.wait(A_HOLD)


class FakePool:
    check_connection = staticmethod(lambda *a, **k: None)
    def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
        self.conn_str = conn_str
        self._conn = FakeConn(conn_str)
    def connection(self):
        conn = self._conn
        class _Ctx:
            def __enter__(self_): return conn
            def __exit__(self_, *a): return False
        return _Ctx()
    def close(self): pass


class FakeSaver:
    def __init__(self, arg): self.arg = arg
    def setup(self): pass


def _mod(name, **attrs):
    m = types.ModuleType(name)
    for k, v in attrs.items():
        setattr(m, k, v)
    sys.modules[name] = m


_mod("langgraph")
_mod("langgraph.checkpoint")
_mod("langgraph.checkpoint.postgres", PostgresSaver=FakeSaver)
_mod("langgraph.checkpoint.memory", MemorySaver=type("MemorySaver", (), {}))
_mod("psycopg")
_mod("psycopg.rows", dict_row=object())
_mod("psycopg_pool", ConnectionPool=FakePool)
cp.atexit = types.SimpleNamespace(register=lambda fn: None)

timings = {}


def boot(label, conn_str):
    os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = conn_str
    # re-resolve per thread: env is process-global so pin via closure instead
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    # call the real path but force conn_str via monkeypatching resolver
    cp.get_postgres_connection_string = lambda cs=conn_str: cs
    cp.get_checkpointer(use_memory=False)
    timings[label] = time.monotonic() - t0


# Thread A bootstraps dbA (will block on advisory lock for A_HOLD seconds)
tA = threading.Thread(target=boot, args=("A", "postgresql://localhost:5432/dbA"))
tA.start()
time.sleep(0.05)  # ensure A grabs _init_lock first
tB0 = time.monotonic()
tB = threading.Thread(target=boot, args=("B", "postgresql://localhost:5432/dbB"))
tB.start()
tB.join()
b_wall = time.monotonic() - tB0
tA.join()

print(f"db_A bootstrap took: {timings.get('A'):.3f}s (the stuck advisory-lock wait)")
print(f"db_B bootstrap wall: {b_wall:.3f}s (UNRELATED database)")
print(f"db_B blocked behind db_A: {'YES' if b_wall > A_HOLD * 0.7 else 'NO'}")

content.text: There's a flaw: I patch `get_postgres_connection_string` from inside the thread, which races. Let me fix by making the resolver return based on a thread-local / env set before the lock. Actually simpler: patch the resolver once to read the env, but env is global. Let me instead bypass `get_postgres_connection_string` entirely by having each thread pass its conn_str through a thread-local.

input.old_string:
timings = {}


def boot(label, conn_str):
    os.environ["ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL"] = conn_str
    # re-resolve per thread: env is process-global so pin via closure instead
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    # call the real path but force conn_str via monkeypatching resolver
    cp.get_postgres_connection_string = lambda cs=conn_str: cs
    cp.get_checkpointer(use_memory=False)
    timings[label] = time.monotonic() - t0
input.new_string:
timings = {}
_tls = threading.local()
cp.get_postgres_connection_string = lambda: _tls.conn_str


def boot(label, conn_str):
    _tls.conn_str = conn_str
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    cp.get_checkpointer(use_memory=False)
    timings[label] = time.monotonic() - t0

db_B bootstrap wall: 0.994s (UNRELATED database)
db_B blocked behind db_A: YES

content.text:
PoC confirms the head-of-line blocking: db_B (unrelated conn_str) is blocked ~0.99s behind db_A's stuck advisory-lock wait, because `_init_lock` is a single process-global mutex held across the blocking `pool.connection()` + advisory lock + `setup()`.

Now the critical severity question: **is this exploitable/realistic in production?** Two conditions must both hold:
1. Multiple distinct conn_strs bootstrapping concurrently in one process (the cross-DB claim), OR same conn_str with a genuinely stuck peer (the indefinite-hang claim).
2. `pg_advisory_lock` actually blocks unboundedly.

Let me assess each. First, does the engine ever use multiple conn_strs in one process? The conn_str comes solely from `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`, resolved fresh each call. Let me confirm there's no multi-tenant / per-study DB selection.

src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py:10:Backends (langgraph-checkpoint-postgres, psycopg, qdrant-client) are optional (the
src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py:18:    get_postgres_connection_string,
src/assay_engine/persistence/__init__.py:32:    "get_postgres_connection_string",
---run_study signature---
def run_study(
    plan: StudyPlan,
    *,
    gate_handler: GateHandler,
    tracer: Tracer | None = None,
    tracker: ExperimentTracker | None = None,
    versioner: DataVersioner | None = None,
    clock: Clock | None = None,
    secret: bytes | None = None,
    trail: ProvenanceTrail | None = None,
) -> StudyResult:
    """Run one study end-to-end through its governed phase sequence. See module docstring.

    ``gate_handler`` is **required** (no default), so governance review is a conscious choice —
    pass :func:`auto_approve` to opt out of human review explicitly, or an operator handler that
    can block/park. ``tracer`` defaults to :class:`OtelTracer` (real spans if OpenTelemetry + a
    provider are present, a graceful no-op otherwise). ``tracker`` (optional) logs params/metrics
    to an experiment store, best-effort (a tracker failure never aborts the run). ``versioner``
    (optional) content-addresses the source so the trail cites retrievable bytes. ``clock``
    injects a deterministic clock for the provenance timestamps only (ordering checks always use
    real time). ``secret`` keys the internal provenance trail with HMAC (forgery-resistant);
    mutually exclusive with passing your own ``trail``. ``trail`` may be a caller-owned
    :class:`ProvenanceTrail` so a run that *raises* still leaves the caller the partial trail.

    Raises ``IngestionError`` on a bad source, ``FirewallViolation``/``PreRegistrationError`` on
    a methodological breach, ``GateError`` if a gate blocks, and ``ValueError`` on a malformed run.
    """
    tracer = tracer or OtelTracer()
    if trail is not None and secret is not None:
        raise ValueError("pass either a caller-owned trail OR a secret to key a new one, not both")
    defn = plan.definition
    modes = defn.modes
    required = required_phases(modes)
    trail = trail if trail is not None else ProvenanceTrail(secret=secret, clock=clock)
    visited: list[Phase] = []
    run_id: str | None = None

    def enter(phase: Phase, **payload: Any) -> None:
        visited.append(phase)
        trail.record("phase", f"enter {phase.name}", phase=phase.name, **payload)

    def track(fn: Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]) -> None:
        # observability is best-effort: a tracker failure is recorded but never aborts a run.
        if tracker is None or run_id is None:
            return
        try:
            fn(tracker, run_id)

content.text:
The conn_str is process-global from a single env var. There is no multi-DB / multi-tenant path in the engine — within one process, every `get_checkpointer` call resolves to the **same** conn_str. So the "blocks initialization for entirely UNRELATED databases" scenario requires the operator to mutate `ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL` between concurrent calls, which is not a real code path. The cross-database angle is largely theoretical for this engine.

The remaining real scenario is the **same-conn_str** case: concurrent `get_checkpointer` calls (the engine's documented concurrent path, tested at line 149) where one holds `_init_lock` while blocked on a genuinely stuck advisory lock. Let me assess whether `pg_advisory_lock` can actually hang unboundedly here.

Critical detail: the advisory lock is taken on a **freshly checked-out pool connection** (line 197), and `setup()` is the DDL between lock/unlock. The only thing that holds `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY` is another process running this exact same bootstrap. The lock is held only for the duration of `setup()` (idempotent DDL — fast, milliseconds typically). So a peer would have to be stuck *inside its own setup()* (e.g., a hung DDL on a locked table) to hold the advisory lock long. That's possible but narrow.

But note: with `_init_lock` held the whole time, even within a single process, ALL concurrent `get_checkpointer` calls serialize on bootstrap. That's by design (once-per-conn). The blocking concern reduces to: a stuck *peer process* on the advisory lock freezes this process's threads. Let me verify whether `pool.connection()` itself can block (pool exhaustion) while holding `_init_lock`. The pool has `max_size=8`; `pool.connection()` blocks waiting for a free connection if all 8 are checked out. Let me check the connection-context release.

The bootstrap uses `with pool.connection()` which returns the conn at block end. Since it's the first op after pool open (min_size=1), a connection is available. Not a real exhaustion risk at bootstrap.

Let me check whether `ConnectionPool` has a default acquire timeout, and whether `pg_advisory_lock` could be replaced with bounded variant — to gauge how justified the "indefinite" claim is. Let me also confirm there's no statement_timeout configured.

---psycopg_pool default timeout---
(self, conninfo: 'ConninfoParam' = '', *, connection_class: 'type[CT]' = <class 'psycopg.Connection'>, kwargs: 'KwargsParam | None' = None, min_size: 'int' = 4, max_size: 'int | None' = None, open: 'bool | None' = None, configure: 'ConnectionCB[CT] | None' = None, check: 'ConnectionCB[CT] | None' = None, reset: 'ConnectionCB[CT] | None' = None, name: 'str | None' = None, close_returns: 'bool' = False, timeout: 'float' = 30.0, max_waiting: 'int' = 0, max_lifetime: 'float' = 3600.0, max_idle: 'float' = 600.0, reconnect_timeout: 'float' = 300.0, reconnect_failed: 'ConnectFailedCB | None' = None, num_workers: 'int' = 3)

content.text:
Confirmed facts for the assessment:

- The pool `connection()` acquire has a 30s default `timeout` (so `pool.connection()` itself won't hang forever).
- But `conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)")` is a plain SQL execute on the connection — there is **no `lock_timeout`/`statement_timeout` set** and no `pg_try_advisory_lock`. If a peer process holds `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY`, this `execute` **blocks indefinitely** at the Postgres level. This part of the claim is technically correct.
- `_init_lock` is held across this potentially-unbounded `execute` (lines 195-211). So a stuck peer freezes this process's bootstrap for all threads. Correct.

Now let me weigh severity realistically:

1. **Cross-database HOL blocking** (the verifier's headline): NOT a real scenario in this engine — single process-global conn_str from one env var, no multi-DB path. The PoC only reproduces it by injecting two conn_strs that the real code never produces concurrently. So the "stalls ALL checkpointer init even for unrelated databases" framing overstates it.

2. **Same-conn_str indefinite hang on a stuck peer**: technically real but requires a peer process stuck *inside its own `setup()` DDL* while holding the advisory lock. `setup()` is idempotent, fast DDL; the realistic stuck-peer window is small. Once `_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` is populated (after first success), the locked section is skipped entirely — bootstrap contention only exists on the very first init per process per conn_str.

3. The unbounded `pg_advisory_lock` (no `lock_timeout`, no try-variant) is the genuinely defensible defect: in a degraded multi-process deployment, a crashed peer that died mid-`setup()` could leave the lock... but advisory locks auto-release on session/connection close, and the pool's connection returning auto-releases it (per the code comment). A truly *crashed* process's connection gets reaped by Postgres, releasing the lock. So "indefinite" requires a peer alive-but-wedged inside setup() — narrow.

Let me verify the auto-release claim and that there's no scenario where the lock leaks across a crash by checking the comment's accuracy. Advisory session locks release when the backend connection terminates — standard Postgres behavior. A crashed peer's TCP connection eventually closes (tcp_keepalives / Postgres detects), releasing the lock. So genuine indefinite hangs require a live-but-stuck peer.

Let me confirm there are no other callers that could create distinct conn_strs (e.g., tests aside, any production multi-process orchestrator).

input.command: cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && grep -rn "multiprocess\|ProcessPool\|fork\|spawn\|Process(" src/ --include="*.py" | head; echo "---any per-study db override?---"; grep -rn "POSTGRES\|dbname\|database=" src/ --include="*.py" | grep -v checkpoint.py | head

content.text:
No multiprocess spawning in the engine and no per-study DB override. Multi-process contention would only arise if the operator runs multiple `assay` processes against the same Postgres (a plausible production deployment, e.g., several concurrent `run_study` processes).

I have enough to render a verdict. Let me synthesize.

**Verdict: The defect is REAL but the verifier's headline (cross-database HOL blocking) is overstated; the genuine, correctly-diagnosed core is the unbounded `pg_advisory_lock` held under a process-global mutex.**

Confirmed true:
- `_init_lock` is a single process-global mutex (line 59) held across blocking I/O including `conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock")` (lines 195-198), which has **no `lock_timeout` and is not `pg_try_advisory_lock`** — so it blocks indefinitely at the PG level if a peer process holds `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY`.
- My PoC drove the real `get_checkpointer` and showed a second thread blocked ~0.99s behind the first's advisory-lock wait.

Overstated / incorrect parts:
- "even for unrelated databases / conn_strs": the engine resolves conn_str from a single process-global env var with no multi-DB path, so concurrent distinct-conn_str bootstrap is not a real code path. My PoC had to inject two conn_strs the real code never produces concurrently. Also, Postgres advisory locks are per-database, so distinct databases wouldn't even contend at the PG layer — only the in-process `_init_lock` would serialize them.
- "indefinitely": auto-releases on connection close; a *crashed* peer's lock is reclaimed when Postgres reaps the backend. A true hang needs a peer alive-but-wedged inside its own fast, idempotent `setup()` DDL — a narrow window that exists only on first-init per process.

Net real risk: multiple concurrent `assay` processes (same DB), and one wedges inside `setup()`, would stall every other process's *first* checkpointer init (and within a process, all threads). Bounded in practice by: once-per-process guard (only first init contends), advisory auto-release, and the 30s pool acquire timeout on `pool.connection()`. Recommended hardening (a bounded lock — `lock_timeout`/`pg_try_advisory_lock` with retry — and/or per-conn_str in-process lock) is sound and cheap. Severity: LOW (production-readiness improvement, narrow trigger, bounded blast radius), not MEDIUM.

input.reasoning:
PARTIALLY CONFIRMED, but the headline is overstated; I recalibrate MEDIUM -> LOW.

What is genuinely true (verified against src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:59,195-211):
- `_init_lock` IS a single process-global `threading.Lock()` (line 59), and it IS held across blocking cross-process I/O: `pool.connection()` (197), `conn.execute("SELECT pg_advisory_lock(%s)")` (198), and `PostgresSaver(...).setup()` DDL (203).
- The advisory lock is taken via a plain `conn.execute` with NO `lock_timeout`/`statement_timeout` and is NOT `pg_try_advisory_lock`. So at the Postgres level this `execute` blocks for as long as a peer process holds `_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY` (a single fixed constant, line 53). That blocking-while-holding-a-global-mutex structure is real.
- My PoC (driving the REAL get_checkpointer with fake langgraph/psycopg, advisory-lock execute made to block) reproduced head-of-line blocking: a second bootstrap thread was blocked ~0.99s behind the first thread's stuck advisory-lock wait.

Why the severity is LOWER than reported (the "stalls ALL init even for unrelated databases, indefinitely" framing is overstated):
1. NO multi-conn_str path exists. conn_str is resolved ONLY from the single process-global env var ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL (line 129); there is no per-study/multi-tenant DB selection, no multiprocess spawning in the engine (grep found none). So within one process every get_checkpointer resolves to the SAME conn_str. The "unrelated databases blocked" scenario is not a real code path — my PoC had to inject two conn_strs the engine never produces concurrently. Additionally, Postgres advisory locks are per-database, so distinct DBs would not even contend at the PG layer.
2. NOT truly indefinite. pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped and auto-releases when the connection closes/returns to the pool (and a CRASHED peer's backend is reaped by Postgres, releasing the lock). A real hang requires a peer that is alive-but-wedged INSIDE its own setup() — which is idempotent, fast DDL — a narrow window.
3. Blast radius is bounded by the once-per-process-per-conn guard (line 196): contention exists ONLY on the very first init per process per conn_str; after _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS is populated the locked section is skipped entirely. And pool.connection() itself has a 30s default acquire timeout.

Net: a real, correctly-located robustness gap (unbounded advisory lock held under a process-wide mutex) that, in a degraded multi-process deployment (several assay processes on one DB, one wedged inside setup()), can stall other processes' FIRST checkpointer init. The suggested fix (bounded lock via lock_timeout / pg_try_advisory_lock+retry, and/or per-conn_str in-process lock) is sound and cheap. But the trigger is narrow, blast radius is bounded, and the cross-database/indefinite framing does not hold for this engine — hence LOW, not MEDIUM.

content.text:
Verdict: REAL but recalibrated MEDIUM → LOW.

The verifier correctly located a genuine robustness gap — `_init_lock` (a single process-global mutex, checkpoint.py:59) is held across an unbounded `pg_advisory_lock` execute (line 198, no `lock_timeout`, not `pg_try_advisory_lock`) plus `setup()` DDL. My PoC driving the real `get_checkpointer` confirmed the head-of-line blocking structure (a second bootstrap thread blocked ~0.99s behind the first's stuck advisory-lock wait).

But the MEDIUM framing ("stalls ALL init even for unrelated databases, indefinitely") is overstated for this engine:
- No multi-conn_str code path exists — conn_str comes from a single process-global env var (`ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`, line 129), no per-study/multi-tenant DB selection, no multiprocess spawning. The "unrelated databases" PoC required injecting conn_strs the engine never produces concurrently; also PG advisory locks are per-database so distinct DBs wouldn't contend at the PG layer anyway.
- Not truly indefinite — advisory locks auto-release on connection close, and a crashed peer's backend is reaped by Postgres. A real hang needs a peer alive-but-wedged inside its own fast, idempotent `setup()`.
- Blast radius bounded by the once-per-process-per-conn guard (line 196): contention exists only on first init; pool acquire itself has a 30s timeout.

The suggested fix (bounded lock via `lock_timeout`/`pg_try_advisory_lock`+retry, and/or per-conn_str in-process lock) is sound and cheap hardening, but the narrow trigger and bounded blast radius put this at LOW.

Relevant file: `/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` (lines 53, 59, 195-211). PoC: `/tmp/poc_init_lock.py`.
